title: National population projections: 2022(base)–2073 | Stats NZ
content:
## National population projections: 2022(base)–2073
27 July 2022, 10:45am
National population projections indicate the future population usually living in New Zealand, and indicate probable outcomes based on different combinations of fertility, mortality, and migration assumptions.
27 November 2024 : We have added ‘Suitable indication of future population change from 2022-base national population projections: Advice to users (November 2024)’ Excel table under download data.
This advice is to assist users of projections with how the 2022-base projections by age group are tracking, until new 2024-base national population projections are published in mid-2025.
The advice is based on the latest available National population estimates release (published November 2024, which does not incorporate revisions from the 2023 Census of Population and Dwellings or from the 2023 Post-enumeration Survey) and 2023 Census results.
Subnational population projections: 2018(base)–2048 update has a similar additional Excel table: ‘Suitable indication of future population change from 2018-base subnational population projections update: Advice to users (November 2024)’.
- Suitable indication of future population change from 2022-base national population projections: Advice to users (November 2024) Excel spreadsheet, 28 KB

### Suitable indication of future population change from 2022-base national population projections: Advice to users (November 2024)
Excel spreadsheet, 28 KB
- National population projections: 2022(base)–2073 Excel spreadsheet, 51 KB

### National population projections: 2022(base)–2073
Excel spreadsheet, 51 KB
National population projections give an indication of New Zealand's future resident population.
In the short term, the projections indicate:
- annual population growth has a 50 percent probability of being between 0.1 and 0.7 percent in 2023 and between 0.5 and 1.2 percent in 2028
- New Zealand's population (5.13 million in 2022) has a 90 percent probability of increasing to between 5.17 and 5.55 million in 2028 and to between 5.28 and 5.85 million in 2033.
In the long term, the projections indicate:
- increasing numbers and proportions of the population at the older ages
- the population aged 65+ (0.79 million in 2020) has a 90 percent probability of increasing to between 1.34 and 1.46 million in 2048 and to between 1.65 and 2.06 million in 2073
- the proportion of the population aged 65+ (16 percent in 2022) has a 90 percent probability of increasing to between 21 and 25 percent in 2048 and between 24 and 32 percent in 2073
- the population aged 85+ (93,000 in 2022) has a 90 percent probability of increasing to between 251,000 and 305,000 in 2048 and to between 322,000 and 491,000 in 2073
- population growth will slow as New Zealand’s population ages and the gap between the number of births and deaths narrows
- New Zealand's population (5.13 million in 2022) has a 90 percent probability of being between 5.55 and 6.65 million in 2048 and between 5.62 and 7.86 million in 2073.
Text alternative for New Zealand population, year ended June 1953 – 2073
Text alternative for Age distribution of population, year ended June 1953 – 2073
- Important advice for using projections
- Slowing population growth
- Narrowing gap between births and deaths
- Number of children remains under one million
- More people aged 15–64 years
- Fastest growth at older ages
- More aged 65+ relative to those aged 15–64
- Oldest ten percent getting older
- Additional 'what if' scenarios
- Projection assumptions
- Accuracy of projections

## Important advice for using projections
National population projections give an indication of the future population usually living in New Zealand. They indicate possible outcomes based on different combinations of fertility , mortality , and migration assumptions. The projections assume current policy settings and do not try to anticipate future major policy changes that may affect population change.
These projections are not predictions. The projections are designed to meet both short-term and long-term planning needs but are not designed to be exact forecasts or to project specific annual variation. They should be used as an indication of the overall trend, rather than as exact forecasts. The projections are updated every 2–3 years to maintain their relevance and usefulness by incorporating new information about demographic trends and developments in methods.
The following results highlight the main trends from the projections. For more detail on these latest projections go to NZ.Stat .
This release contains 2022-base projections of the population usually living in New Zealand. These supersede the 2020-base projections released in December 2020. The new projections have the provisional estimated resident population at 30 June 2022 as a base and cover the period 2023–2073 at one-year intervals. Email demography@stats.govt.nz for extended projections beyond 2073.

### Which projection should I use?
Users can make their own judgement as to which projections are most suitable for their purposes. At the time of release, the median projection (50th percentile) indicates an estimated 50 percent chance that the actual value will be lower and a 50 percent chance that the actual value will be higher than this percentile. Other percentiles indicate the distribution of values (such as projection results or assumptions). For example, the 25th percentile indicates an estimated 25 percent chance that the actual value will be lower and a 75 percent chance that the actual value will be higher than this percentile. Shading in graphs indicates the chance that actual values will fall within a certain range. Different shading is used to distinguish different ranges.

## Slowing population growth
Growth is likely to slow in the long term. There is roughly a 1 in 4 chance that there will be no growth in the population by the 2050s, and a population decline by the 2060s.
Text alternative for Annual population growth rate, year ended June 1953–2073

## Narrowing gap between births and deaths
The narrowing gap between births and deaths is driving slower population growth. Births are likely to fluctuate from year to year, but average about 60,000 a year over the coming decades. From an estimate of 61,600 in 2022, it is likely annual births will number 55,000–65,000 through the 2020s.
There is, however, considerable uncertainty in the number of births. Future birth numbers depend on the number of women of childbearing age and their fertility rates – how many children they have and the timing of their births. By 2073, there is roughly a 1 in 4 chance that annual births could exceed 74,000, and a similar chance that they could be under 48,000.
Text alternative for Births, year ended June 1953–2073
Text alternative for Deaths, year ended June 1953–2073
The future number of deaths is more certain. Deaths are expected to increase steadily despite assumed lower death rates and increasing life expectancy. From an estimated 35,100 deaths in the year ended June 2022, it is likely that annual deaths will exceed 40,000 by the early 2030s and exceed 50,000 by the early 2040s. Deaths will rise as more people reach the older ages where most deaths occur. Currently, nearly 4 in 5 male deaths and 5 in 6 female deaths, occur at ages 65 and over (65+).
With deaths rising faster than births, annual natural increase (births minus deaths) is likely to decrease. From 27,000 in 2022, there is roughly a 3 in 4 chance that annual natural increase will be under 20,000 by the mid-2030s. By 2044, there is roughly a 1 in 4 chance of natural decrease – more deaths than births.
Text alternative for Natural increase, year ended June 1953–2073
Text alternative for Median age, year ended June 1953–2073
Significant changes in the age structure of the population will continue. The median age (where half the population is younger and half is older than this age) of New Zealand’s population increased from 25.6 years in 1970 to 37.6 years in 2013. It has hovered at just over 37 years since then as net migration – concentrated at ages 18–39 years – contributed significantly to population growth.
However, the upwards trajectory of median age is projected to resume. A median age of 40 years is likely to be reached in the early 2030s. By 2073, there is a 3 in 4 chance that half of the population will be older than 45 years. The gradual ageing reflects the combined effect of people having fewer children and people living longer. This impact is accentuated by the large number of people born between 1950 and the early 1970s moving into the older ages.
Population ageing is not caused by the baby boomers (see Population – concepts on Datainfo+ for definition), but by the transition to lower birth rates and lower death rates. The projections indicate that once the baby boomers have moved through the age structure, the New Zealand population will not revert to a younger age structure – barring major changes in childbearing patterns (fertility rates).
Text alternative for Population pyramids by age and sex, years ended 1967, 2020, 2048, and 2073

## Number of children remains under one million
The number of children aged 0–14 years peaked at 940,000 in 1974, decreased steadily to 770,000 in 1989, and then rebounded to 965,000 in 2022. The future number of children is uncertain, but the median projection indicates the number could drop gradually to 911,000 in the mid-to-late-2030s and subsequently fluctuate around 930,000. Projections of the number of children are less certain than those for older age groups because the number of future births is uncertain.
Even if the number of children increases, it is unlikely to increase as fast as the older segment of the population. As a result, the proportion of the population under 15 years is likely to decrease. From 1 in 3 of the population during the early 1960s, and just under 1 in 5 of the population in 2022, it is highly likely that children will account for less than 1 in 5 of the population throughout the projection period (2022–2073).
Population by broad age group (based on the median projection), year ended June 1953–2073

## More people aged 15–64 years
The number of people aged 15–64 years more than doubled between 1963 and 2022 to 3.33 million. It is projected to grow gradually, with the median projection indicating 3.74 million in 2048 and 3.87 million in 2073. In 2073, those aged 15–64 years would make up 58 percent of the total population, down from 65 percent in 2022.
The number of people aged 40–64 years increased rapidly during the 1990s and 2000s as the baby boomers moved into this age group. The increase slowed during the 2010s and into the 2020s as the number of people entering the age group only slightly exceeds those leaving it. The median projection indicates that the number of people in this age group (1.59 million in 2022) will increase to 1.92 million in 2048, and 2.05 million in 2073. In 2073, 31 percent of the population will be aged 40–64 years, down from a peak of 33 percent in 2012.
The median projection indicates that the number of people aged 15–39 years (1.73 million in 2022) will increase to 1.83 million in 2048, drop slightly, then rise again to 1.82 million in 2073. This age group accounted for about 41 percent of the population in the mid-1980s and 34 percent of the population in 2022. It is expected to account for only 30 percent in 2048, and 27 percent in 2073.

## Fastest growth at older ages
The number of people aged 65+ doubled between 1994 and 2020, to reach 842,000. The number is projected to double again by 2063. There is an estimated 90 percent probability that there will be 1.34–1.46 million people aged 65+ in 2048, and 1.65–2.06 million in 2073. The largest growth occurs between 2011 and 2038 as the baby boomers move into the 65+ age group.
By 2028, it is expected that 19–20 percent of New Zealanders will be aged 65+, compared with 16 percent in 2022. By 2048, this proportion is expected to reach 21–25 percent, and 24–32 percent by 2073.
Text alternative for Population aged 65+ and 85+, year ended June 1953–2073
Text alternative for Percentage of population aged 65+ and 85+, year ended June 1953–2073
Within the 65+ age group, the number of people aged 85 and over (85+) is expected to increase significantly. It is expected that 251,000–305,000 people will be aged 85+ in 2048, and 322,000–491,000 in 2073, up from 93,000 in 2022. By the end of the 2040s, about 1 in 5 people aged 65+ will be 85+, compared with around 1 in 9 in 2022.

## More aged 65+ relative to those aged 15–64
Dependency ratios relate the number of people in the ‘dependent’ age groups (defined here as 0–14 and 65+ years) to the working-age population (15–64 years). They indicate changes in New Zealand’s age structure. However, because of their simplicity, dependency ratios do not allow for some people in the working-age population to not be in the workforce, and for some people aged 65+ to be in the workforce. For those aged 65+, the term ‘dependency’ does not necessarily imply financial or economic dependency – people are progressively living longer, are healthier, and are working longer.
The 65+ dependency ratio (the number of people aged 65+ per 100 people aged 15–64 years) increased gradually from 14 per 100 in the mid-1960s to 25 per 100 in 2022. It is projected to increase significantly, with the ratio expected to be in the range of 31–35 per 100 in 2033, 34–39 per 100 in 2048, and 41–56 per 100 in 2073. This means that for every person aged 65+, there will be about 3.0 people aged 15–64 in 2033, 2.7 in 2048, and 2.1 in 2073. This compares with 4.0 people in 2022 and 7.1 in the mid-1960s.
In contrast, the 0–14 dependency ratio (the number of people aged 0–14 years per 100 people aged 15–64 years) decreased from a peak of 57 per 100 in 1961 to 29 per 100 in 2022. This downward trend is likely to continue, with the ratio expected to be in the range of 24–28 per 100 in 2033, 19–30 per 100 in 2048, and 16–32 per 100 in 2073.
Text alternative for Ratio of 65+ to 15–64 population, year ended June 1953–2073
Text alternative for Ratio of 0-14 to 15–64 population, year ended June 1953–2073
The total dependency ratio (sum of the 0–14 and 65+ dependency ratios) reached its lowest level since the mid-1930s in 2006 (50 per 100). It is projected to increase from 54 per 100 in 2022, to be in the range of 56–62 per 100 in 2033, 57–68 per 100 in 2048, and 65–79 per 100 in 2073. The 65+ dependency ratio will then contribute more than three-fifths to the total dependency ratio, compared with two-fifths in 2022. A total dependency ratio of over 70 per 100 was also experienced around 1960, when the 65+ dependency ratio contributed about one-fifth to the total dependency ratio.
Text alternative for Ratio of 0–14 and 65+ to 15–64 population, year ended June 1953–2073

## Oldest ten percent getting older
The age of the oldest 10 percent of the population is also projected to rise significantly. In 2022, 10 percent of the population is over 71.6 years. It is projected that the age of the oldest 10 percent of the population will increase to 74.5–75.5 years in 2033, 77.3–79.4 years in 2048, and 79.1–82.8 years in 2073.
Text alternative for Oldest 10 percent of the population, year ended June 1953–2073

## Additional 'what if?' scenarios
The projections discussed above indicate probable outcomes based on different combinations of fertility, mortality, and migration assumptions. Five additional projections were derived to explore other scenarios of interest.
The median projection indicates the population will increase by about 1.5 million between 2022 and 2073 to 6.6 million, but as illustrated by the scenarios below, population growth would be higher if fertility, life expectancy, or net migration (migrant arrivals minus migrant departures) were higher. Median assumptions are used in these scenarios, aside from the one assumption being explored in each scenario.

### What if fertility was higher?
The population would reach seven million in the mid-to-late-2050s and eight million in the early 2070s with a total fertility rate of 2.3 births per woman (very high fertility, see Fertility on Datainfo+ for definitions). The very high fertility scenario would produce a much younger age structure compared with all other scenarios.
With a total fertility rate of 2.3 births per woman, births would continue to outnumber deaths – by 29,000 in 2048 and 44,000 in 2073. There would be 111,000 births in 2073 under this scenario, compared with 61,000 under the median projection. The number of children would rise 63 percent over the projection period, compared with a four percent drop under the median projection. Population ageing would continue but at a much slower rate, with the median age increasing from 38.1 years in 2022, peaking at 40.7 in the late-2030s and early-2050s, before easing to 39.9 in 2073. By comparison, in the median projection, the median age increases steadily to 47.1 years in 2073.
The very high fertility scenario results in a lower 65+ dependency ratio in 2073 – 40 per 100 compared with 48 per 100 in the median projection. However, the 0–14 dependency ratio would be much higher – 34 per 100 compared with 24 per 100 in the median projection. The total dependency ratio would therefore be only slightly higher – 74 per 100 compared with 72 per 100 in the median projection.

### What if migration was higher?
The population would reach seven million by 2053 with net migration of 50,000 a year (very high migration), which is earlier than in the very high fertility scenario.
It would also slow the ageing of the population, but much less than the very high fertility scenario. The median age would increase to 45.6 years in 2073 with very high net migration – only slightly lower than the median projection (47.1 years). This reflects that migrants inevitably age as well.
There would be 24 percent more people, 28 percent more births, and nine percent more deaths in 2073 than in the median projection. By 2073, births would exceed deaths by 6,000.

### What if life expectancy was higher?
The median projection assumes recent reductions in age-specific death rates continue over the projection period. Very low mortality, with an increase in life expectancy at birth of about 0.3 years of life per year (compared with 0.1 for median life expectancy), would result in an average life expectancy of 96.0 years for both males and females in 2073. In this scenario, the population would reach 7.1 million in 2073. This is 425,000 more people than under the median projection. The population for those aged 65 years will nearly triple in size to 2.2 million in 2073 (396,000 more than the median projection). The 85+ age group would increase to 710,000 in 2073 (306,000 more than the median projection).
With more people in the older ages, the population would age even faster than in the median projection – the median age of the population would be over 47 years in the early 2060s. The 65+ dependency ratio would be higher from early 2030s compared with the median projection. Annual deaths would still increase over the projection period but would be about 14,000 less than the median projection in 2073.

### What if there was no migration?
An interesting projection for comparative purposes is to assume no migrant arrivals and no migrant departures. This shows how the population is affected by births and deaths. With no migration, the population would peak at just under 5.4 million in the early 2040s then slowly decline as deaths outnumber births. In the mid-2060s, the population would return to a similar size as in 2022 and continue to decline to just under 5 million in 2073. Compared with the median projection, the population would be lower in all age groups, but the median age and 65+ dependency ratio would be higher.

### What if migration fluctuated?
The stochastic projections assume that net migration varies each year, with the median assumption equating to 25,000 a year from 2026. The cyclic migration scenario assumes annual net migration fluctuates specifically between -5,000 and +60,000 on a 10-year cycle, with an average of 25,000.
The population (in the cyclic migration scenario) in 2073 is 92,000 less than the median projection. However, due to annual differences in net migration, the largest population difference is 96,400 during the projection period. Other characteristics of the population (for example, age distribution, dependency ratios, births, and deaths) are very similar to the median projection. This scenario therefore indicates that a constant level of net migration in the long term is a sufficient assumption for most purposes.

## Projection assumptions
Projection assumptions are formulated after analysing short-term and long-term historical trends, recent trends and patterns observed in other countries, and government policy.

### Main changes since the previous 2020-base projections
Deriving the projections involved a review of all projection assumptions. The following are the main changes from the previous 2020-base projections.
- The base population is 5.128 million at 30 June 2022 (2018-base population estimates). This is 45,300 (0.9 percent) lower than the 2020-base median projection for 2022.
- The TFR in the short term is assumed to be 1.7 births per woman in 2023 (up from 1.65 for the 2020-base median), then gradually decreasing to 1.65 by 2042. The long-term median period total fertility rate (TFR) is assumed to be 1.65 births per woman, the same as the 2020-base median assumption.
- Age-specific death rates continue to decrease, but this decrease is more gradual compared with the 2020-base median projections. Life expectancy at birth for males and females will increase from 80.8 and 84.1 years in 2023 to 87.0 and 89.7 years in 2073. This is 0.5 years lower than the 2020-base median assumptions.
- COVID-19 had a significant impact on international travel and migration in 2020–2021. With the easing of New Zealand border restrictions through 2022, net migration is assumed to gradually rise from the current negative levels.
- In the short term, median annual net migration is assumed to be -2,000 in the year ended June 2023, gradually rising to the long-term median net migration of 25,000 in 2026 and beyond.
The base population as at 30 June 2022 is 5.128 million. It incorporates results from the 2018 Census of Population and Dwellings and the 2018 Post-enumeration Survey. This base population is 45,300 (0.9 percent) lower than the 2020-base median projection for 2022, mainly reflecting the low net migration during June 2021 and 2022 years.
See Estimated resident population (2018-base): At 30 June 2018 for more information about the base population.
The ERP is the best available measure of the number of people usually living in New Zealand. However, for projection purposes, some uncertainty in the base population has been assumed. This uncertainty is assumed to vary by age and sex and arises from the following three broad sources.
- Census enumeration and processing: Coverage errors may arise from non-enumeration and mis-enumeration (for example, residents counted as visitors from overseas and vice versa), either because of deliberate or inadvertent respondent or collector error. Errors may also arise during census processing (for example, scanning, numeric, and character recognition, imputation, coding, editing, and integration of administrative data).
- Adjustments in deriving population estimates: This includes the adjustments applied in deriving the ERP at 30 June of the census year (for example, net census undercount). It also includes uncertainty associated with the post-censal components of population change (for example, births, deaths, and net migration between the census date and 30 June).
- Migration estimates for more recent months have greater uncertainty and are therefore subject to larger revisions than estimates for earlier periods. Estimated provisional net migration suggested a loss of 10,700 for the year ended May 2022 (ie, more departures than arrivals). These estimates could be revised up or down. See International migration publications for more details.
Simulations of the base population are produced by drawing a random number sampled from a normal distribution with a mean of zero. For each simulation, a random number is multiplied by the assumed standard error for each age-sex then added to the base ERP.
Fertility assumptions are formulated using birth registrations, period and cohort fertility rates, census data on children ever born (including rates of childlessness), and international comparisons.
The median period total fertility rate (TFR) is assumed to be 1.7 in 2023 then gradually decrease to 1.65 by 2042, then stay at 1.65 births per woman throughout the rest of the projection period. The median assumption is not an assumed trajectory, but the median of the fertility simulations that vary every year around that median.
- In the 32 years from 1977 to 2009, the period TFR was generally in the range of 1.9–2.2 births per woman. From 2010, the TFR has dropped from 2.18 to 1.61 in 2021. Data in early 2022 shows a small increase in the TFR, with an estimated June year TFR of 1.7.
- The cohort TFR indicates a progressive decline in completed family size. Women born in the early 1950s averaged 2.5 births each, compared with 2.2 for those born in the early 1970s.
- Census data (1981, 1996, 2006, 2013, and 2018) on the number of children ever born also indicates progressive declines in completed family size and progressive increases in childlessness.
- Internationally, TFRs are generally declining, or are already lower than in New Zealand. New Zealand's TFR has historically been one of the highest among Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development (OECD) countries. Even with the recent decline in New Zealand’s period TFR, it remains above the OECD average.
Text alternative for Period total fertility rate, year ended June 1953–2073
Text alternative for Cohort total fertility rate, 1923–2043
Age-specific fertility rates (ASFRs) are assumed to vary throughout the projection period. The median ASFRs decline for women aged under 35 years and increase for women aged 35 years and over.
Period fertility rates at selected ages, year ended June 1968–2046
Text alternative for Period fertility rates by age, 2042
Simulations of TFR are produced using a simple random walk with drift model. Random errors are sampled from a normal distribution with a mean of zero and a standard deviation of 0.0546. The standard deviation is derived by fitting an autoregressive integrated moving average or ARIMA(0,1,0) model to annual TFR for years ended June 1977–2022 (with 2022 based on estimates). The drift function shifts the median of the TFR simulations to follow the assumed median TFR. Median ASFRs are scaled to sum to the simulated TFR.
Simulations of the sex ratio at birth for each year are produced by drawing a random number sampled from a normal distribution with a mean of 105.5 males per 100 females and a standard deviation of 1.0. The mean and standard deviation are calculated from historical data for the years ended December 1900–2022 (with 2022 based on estimates).
Future fertility trends are uncertain and depend on a range of factors, including:
- changes in population composition and different trends in subpopulations (including ethnic groups)
- trends in ideal family size and the strength of individual desires for children
- trends in the patterns of education and work, including the timing, duration, and proportion of time dedicated to those activities
- changing macro-level conditions (for example, government policies, childcare facilities, and housing) that influence the cost of children in a broad sense
- changing nature and stability of partnerships, including rates of partnership formation (including re-partnering) and dissolution
- changing biomedical conditions (for example, female fecundity, new methods for assisted conception).
Mortality/survival assumptions are formulated using death registrations, period and cohort mortality rates, and international comparisons. Death rates are assumed to vary throughout the projection period, with the assumptions driven by trends in age-sex death rates. Life expectancy assumptions are not explicitly formulated but are derived from the assumed death rates.
Male and female age-specific death rate assumptions are formulated using a coherent functional demographic method (FDM) developed by Hyndman, Booth, and Yasmeen ( Coherent mortality forecasting: The product-​​​​ratio method with functional time series models , 2012). This method builds on the FDM of Hyndman and Ullah ( Robust forecasting of mortality and fertility rates: A functional data approach , 2007), which is itself an extension of the Lee-Carter method widely used in mortality forecasting. The research of the authors and Booth, Hyndman, Tickle, and de Jong ( Lee-Carter mortality forecasting: a multi-country comparison of variants and extensions , 2006) indicates that FDM forecasts are more accurate than the original Lee-Carter method and at least as accurate as several other Lee-Carter variants. The advantage of the coherent FDM is that it ensures male and female assumptions do not diverge over time.
The coherent FDM uses smoothed historical data to fit the model, which is then forecast using ARIMA and autoregressive fractionally integrated moving average (ARFIMA) time-series models. The historical data is derived from Stats NZ’s cohort mortality series, transposed to give period death rates for each age for the years ended June 1967–2021 (see New Zealand cohort tables: March 2022 update ). Simulations of death rates are produced using an ARIMA(0,2,2) model to give plausible uncertainty bounds.
Male death rates at selected ages, year ended June 1953–2073
Female death rates at selected ages, year ended June 1953–2073
The median assumption has a male period life expectancy at birth of 83.9 years in 2045 and 86.8 years in 2070. The corresponding female period life expectancy at birth is 86.9 years in 2045 and 89.4 years in 2070.
Text alternative for Male period life expectancy and female period life expectancy at birth, 1953–2073
The median assumption has a male cohort life expectancy at birth of 78.9 years for those born in 1962 and 88.5 years for those born in 2022. The corresponding female cohort life expectancy at birth is 83.5 years for those born in 1962 and 91.1 years for those born in 2022.
Text alternative for Male cohort life expectancy and female cohort life expectancy at birth, 1900–2022
Despite differences in methods, the New Zealand life expectancy assumptions are broadly consistent with those in other countries.
Although mortality reductions are expected to continue in the future, the extent of the trends is uncertain and depends on these factors:
- changes in population composition and different trends in subpopulations (including ethnic groups)
- changes in biomedical technology, regenerative medicine, and preventative methods including monitoring, treatment, and early intervention
- changes in health care systems including effectiveness of public health
- changes in behaviour and lifestyle (for example, smoking, exercise, and diet)
- changes in infectious diseases and resistance to antibiotics
- environmental change, disasters, and wars.
In May 2022 we conducted a survey to elicit the opinions of migration experts on future net migration levels. This survey was based on an approach developed by Statistics Canada (see 2018 Survey of experts on future demographic trends ). This survey was used for the first time in the development of the 2020 national population projections where it helped inform the migration assumptions. For the 2022 survey we invited input from over 325 different people from central government agencies, central and regional forecasting networks, and population specialists. The responses to that survey have again informed the migration assumptions for these projections.
Survey results show that migration experts expect low net migration levels in the short term (year ended June 2023), with a 50 percent chance of net migration between -10,000 and 5,000. In the medium term (year ended June 2028), respondents project median net migration between 20,000 and 40,000, increasing to between 25,000 and 50,000 in the longer term (2048). The median response, however, was about 30,000 for both 2028 and 2048. Respondents indicated more uncertainty in the long-term net migration, as reflected in a wider range of survey responses.
Elicitation survey median response, net migration, 2023, 2028, and 2048
Elicitation survey median response, 2023, 2028, and 2048 (' low ')
Elicitation survey median response, 2023, 2028, and 2048 (' q1 ')
Elicitation survey median response, 2023, 2028, and 2048 (' median ')
Elicitation survey median response, 2023, 2028, and 2048 (' q3 ')
Elicitation survey median response, 2023, 2028, and 2048 (' high ')
For these 2022-base projections, migration is assumed to vary throughout the projection period. The median net migration (migrant arrivals less migrant departures) assumption is:
- 25,000 in 2026 and beyond.
The median assumption is not an assumed trajectory, but the median of the migration simulations, which vary every year around that median.
The net migration assumptions are broadly consistent with the results of the expert survey. These assumptions show lower net migration gains in the short term that reflect the impact of the COVID-19 pandemic on international travel and migration. Net migration levels are assumed to increase over the next few years, with the assumed long-run median annual net migration of 25,000 reflecting the average annual gain of 15,000–30,000 observed since the late 1980s.
Text alternative text Net migration, 1953–2073
Net migration by age-sex reflects historically observed trends. Generally, net migration is dominated by migrant arrivals and migrant departures at ages 15–38 years. This age-sex distribution of migrants was temporarily affected by COVID-19-related international travel and border restrictions, but as travel restrictions are gradually lifted, it is assumed that the age-sex distribution of migrants will return to pre-COVID-19 patterns.
Text alternative for Annual net migration by age, 2026
Future migration trends are uncertain and depend on a range of factors in source and destination countries, including:
- changes in immigration policy (in New Zealand and other countries)
- changes in the main motives for migration (eg, work, family reunification, education, asylum, and retirement)
- changes in migration pressure in source countries (eg, population growth and economic growth)
- changes in the attractiveness of New Zealand as a place to live (eg, work opportunities, economic conditions, wages relative to costs and other countries, and settlement and integration practices)
- costs of migration, including cost of travel and existence of networks and pathways that facilitate migration
- environmental change, disasters, pandemics, and wars.
Simulations of net migration are produced using an ARIMA(1,0,1) with drift model. The autoregressive and moving average parameters are derived by fitting an ARIMA(1,0,1) model to annual migration for the years ended June 1988–2022. The drift function shifts the median of the net migration simulations to follow the assumed median net migration. Net migration by age-sex is interpolated between a high and low pattern, to sum to the simulated net migration level.
In the long term, there is an assumed 50 percent chance in any year that net migration will be within ±19,000 of the median (25,000), and a 90 percent chance within ±45,000. This compares with ±22,000 and ±55,000 from the 2020-base assumptions.

## Accuracy of projections
The accuracy of these projections is unknown at the time of release. While the assumptions are formulated from an assessment of short-term and long-term demographic trends, there is no certainty that any of the assumptions will be realised. The projections do not take into account non-demographic factors (eg, war, catastrophes, and major government and business decisions) that may invalidate the projections.
See How accurate are population estimates and projections? An evaluation of Statistics New Zealand population estimates and projections, 1996–2013 for an evaluation of previous Stats NZ national and subnational population estimates and projections.
Text alternative for New Zealand population, year ended June 1953–2073
The fan chart shows the total New Zealand population from 1953 to 2073. The total New Zealand population is estimated from 1953 to 2022 and projected after the year ended June 2022. A black line represents the median (50th percentile). The dark shaded area represents the 50 percent range between the 25th and 75th percentiles. This means there is an estimated 50 percent chance that the total New Zealand population will fall within this dark shaded area. The light shaded area below and above the dark shaded area represents the range between 5th and 25th percentiles, and between the 75th and 95th percentiles. This means there is an estimated 90 percent chance that the total New Zealand population will fall within the light and dark shaded areas.

Four lines represent the proportion for broad age groups, 0 – -14, 15 – 39, 40 – 64, and 65+.

National population projections (data collection) – DataInfo+ provides information about projections methodology.
